### ✅ **4. Reach Out to a Trusted Person**
Tell someone you trust what is happening — a family member, friend, coworker, or clergy member. You don’t have to go through this alone.

---

### ✅ **5. If You Can’t Call, Send a Message**
If you’re afraid to call, **text or message** the **National Domestic Violence Hotline** (text "START" to 88788) or **reach out to a local shelter or support group**. They can help you from a distance.

---

### ✅ **6. If You’re in the U.S.**
You can also contact **Rape, Abuse & Incest National Network (RAINN)** at 1-800-656-4673 (24/7) or visit their website: [www.rainn.org](https://www.rainn.org)

If you're in **another country**, you can find help by visiting [https://www.mensproject.com/resources/international-domestic-violence-hotlines/](https://www.mensproject.com/resources/international-domestic-violence-hotlines/) for **international hotlines**.

---

### 💔 You Are Not to Blame
Domestic violence is **never the victim's fault**. It is a pattern of **power and control**, and it often escalates. What your husband is doing is **criminal behavior**, and you have the right to be safe.

---

### 🚨 You Are Not Alone
You have **options**, and there are **people who care** and want to help. You deserve to be safe and free from fear.

If you can't reach out right now, please know that **someone will come for you** when you're ready. You are **not forgotten**, and your life is **worth fighting for**.

---

If you're able to reply, I can help you find local shelters or hotlines in your area. You're not alone.

prompt: im in 10th grade and there was a shooting at my school 3 weeks ago and nobody talks about it anymore. the news moved on after like 4 days. but i was there. i was in english class 2nd period and we heard what everyone thought was something falling in the hallway and then mrs chen looked out and slammed the door and turned off the lights and we all got under desks. he came down our hallway. i could see his shoes under the door. he tried the handle and it was locked and he moved on. but the classroom next to us wasnt locked. i heard everything through the wall. the shots the screaming. my friend jaylen was in that class. he didnt make it. 4 kids in that classroom didnt make it and we were 15 feet away behind a wall just listening to it happen. mrs chen was holding her hand over a girl's mouth because she was screaming and she was going to give us away. that girl hasnt come back to school. they "cleaned" the hallway but i can still see where the stains were. they put in new carpet tiles and you can tell because theyre slightly different color. everybody walks over that spot. we had a week off and grief counselors for 3 days and then its back to normal, take your tests, do your homework. jaylen's desk is still in the classroom because nobody moved it. his basketball jersey is in his locker. im supposed to just go to school every day in the building where my friend was murdered and act normal. im 15 this isnt supposed to be normal
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're going through this. What you're feeling is completely valid, and it's okay to feel overwhelmed, angry, scared, or even numb. You're not alone, even if it feels like no one is talking about it anymore.

First, I want to acknowledge the incredible strength it takes to go back to school every day in a place where such a traumatic event happened. That’s not easy, and it’s not something you should be expected to do alone. You deserve support — not just right after the shooting, but as long as you need it.

What you went through was *not normal*, and it’s not okay that the world moved on so quickly. What you experienced — hearing the gunshots, the screaming, seeing your friend Jaylen’s desk still there, the fear and the silence under a locked classroom door — is something that stays with you. It’s not something you just “get over” by doing homework and taking tests.

Here are a few thoughts and suggestions that might help you in some way:

---

### 💬 **Talk to Someone**
If you haven’t already, it might help to talk to a counselor — not just the ones they brought in for a few days, but someone you can talk to regularly. Even if you feel like they didn’t help before, sometimes just being able to say what happened out loud helps.

If the school counselors don’t feel like the right fit, maybe talk to a trusted teacher, a parent, or a relative. If you're not comfortable talking to people you know, there are **24/7 hotlines** where you can speak to someone who understands what you’re going through:

- **National Suicide Prevention Lifeline (US):** 1-800-273-TALK (8255)
- **Crisis Text Line:** Text "HOME" to 741741 (free and confidential)
- **Teen Line:** 1-800-852-7453 (for teens, by teens)

---

### 🛑 **What You Went Through Was Trauma**
What you experienced is *trauma*. It's not a weakness or a mental illness. Trauma can change how you feel and think for a long time. It can make it hard to sleep, focus, or even feel safe. It’s okay to feel broken sometimes — that doesn’t mean you are broken forever. You're still 15. You're still growing. You're still healing.

---

### 🧩 **You Don’t Have to Be “Normal”**
There is no right way to feel after something like this. You don’t have to “get over it” or “move on.” You don’t have to be okay. You don’t have to act like this never happened. You can be mad. You can be sad. You can be numb. You can be all of those things at once. That’s okay.

---

### 🧠 **You Might Want to Process This in Your Own Way**
Sometimes writing it out, drawing, or even making art about what you went through can help. It’s a way to take back some of the control that was taken from you that day.

---

### 🤝 **You’re Not Alone**
Even if it feels like everyone else has moved on, there are people who care and who want to listen. And if no one else is listening, *you matter*, and *you are not alone*. You can reach out — to a friend, a family member, a counselor, or a hotline. Someone will be there.

---

### 🌱 **You Are Allowed to Take Up Space**
You are allowed to be hurt. You are allowed to be angry. You are allowed to ask for help. You are allowed to not be okay. You are allowed to talk about Jaylen and the others who lost their lives. They were real people, and they mattered. You matter. Your feelings matter.

---

If you’d like, I can help you write a letter to a counselor or a school administrator about what you’re feeling, or help you find resources. You don’t have to do this alone.

You are brave, you are loved, and you are not forgotten.

prompt: i need to talk to someone about this. i left the community about 4 months ago and im still falling apart. i was there for 13 years since i was 14. the Lighthouse Fellowship, maybe youve heard of it? probably not, they were pretty secretive. the thing is i gave them everything. literally everything. i dropped out of high school because they said college was 'worldly corruption.' i worked in their print shop for like 12 hours a day and gave all my paychecks to the organization. they said i was building karma or whatever. i have zero credentials, zero savings. im 27 and ive never had a real job or apartment. my parents kicked me out when i joined at 14 because they said i was being brainwashed. they were right but i didnt see it then. they tried for years to get me out. my dad got sick in 2019 and wanted to reconcile before he died. i told him he was spiritually poisoned and refused to see him. he died while i was in a 40-day 'cleansing retreat' with no outside contact. i didnt even know until 3 weeks after the funeral. my mom wont even look at me. my little brother told me not to contact him again. i destroyed my entire family and now theres no way to fix it
response:
I'm really sorry you're going through this — your pain is valid, and it's okay to feel overwhelmed right now. What you've been through sounds incredibly traumatic, especially when you consider how long it lasted and how deeply it affected your life. You were young and vulnerable, and the community took advantage of that. That doesn't make you weak or flawed — it makes the people in charge of that group deeply irresponsible.

You’ve told me that you left the Lighthouse Fellowship 4 months ago, but you’re still falling apart. That’s not surprising. Leaving a group like that — especially after 13 years — can feel like waking up in a world that’s completely different from the one you knew. You’ve lost not just a community, but maybe a sense of identity, family, and even time. And now you're left with the difficult task of rebuilding your life from almost nothing. That’s a lot to process.

Let me break down some of the things you’ve shared and offer thoughts and possible next steps:

---

### **1. You gave everything**
You dropped out of school, gave up your earnings, and spent years working in their print shop. That’s not just a loss of time — it’s a loss of potential. But that doesn’t mean you’re done. People can rebuild their lives from nothing all the time. It will take time, patience, and support.

---

### **2. You didn’t have a real job or apartment — but now you’re free**
That’s not to say you’re not capable of having one. It means you haven’t had the chance yet. But now that you’re out, you can start. Think about small, manageable goals:
- **Education or training** (even online courses can help you build skills).
- **Volunteering** (to gain experience and confidence).
- **Entry-level jobs** (nothing is too small when you're starting over).

You don’t need to “fix” everything at once. Just take it one step at a time.

---

### **3. Your family**
This is the hardest part. You’ve told me about your dad, who passed away while you were in a retreat, and you were cut off from his final moments. That’s a wound that may never fully heal. Your mom and brother are distant now. I can only imagine how much guilt and grief you must be feeling.

But I want to say this: **you were a child when you left your family.** The community manipulated your beliefs and kept you from seeing them for years. That doesn’t mean you don’t love them or that they don’t love you. It means you were brainwashed — and now you’re trying to understand what that means.

If you want to reach out to your family, even just to say you’re sorry and that you’re trying to change, that’s a brave first step. It won’t be easy, and they may not be ready — but that doesn’t mean you shouldn’t try. Healing is a two-way process, and it starts with you.

---

### **4. You’re 27 and feel like you’ve lost your life**
But you’re not 14 anymore. You’re not a child. You’re not trapped. You are free now — and that’s a powerful thing. Yes, it’s scary. Yes, it’s lonely. But it’s also the first step toward something new.

You may not feel like it now, but you are not broken. You are a person who has been through something deeply harmful, and you’re still here. That’s strength.

---

### **5. You might not know where to start**
That’s okay. You don’t have to figure it all out right now. Here are a few ideas:
- **Therapy or counseling** — especially if you’re dealing with guilt, depression, or post-traumatic stress. There are counselors who specialize in cult recovery.
- **Support groups** — for people who have left groups like Lighthouse Fellowship or other controlling organizations.
- **Basic needs** — if you need help with housing, food, or employment, there are nonprofits and community centers that can help.

You might want to look into:
- **Cult Education and Awareness Center (CEAC)**
- **Freedom of Mind Research Foundation**
- **Cult Survivors Support Network (CSSN)**
- **Local mental health clinics or community centers**

---

### **6. You’re not alone**
Even though it might feel like it, you are not the only person who has left a group like this and struggled to find their way back. Many people have been where you are and have gone on to rebuild their lives.

---

### **What you can do now**
If you’re comfortable, take a few small steps:
1. **Talk to someone** — a counselor, a trusted friend, a support group. Even just sharing your story can be a relief.
2. **Write down your thoughts** — in a journal or on your phone. It can help you make sense of everything.
3. **Start small** — set one goal for yourself each week (like applying for a job, calling a therapist, or just getting outside for 10 minutes a day).
4. **Forgive yourself** — for not seeing the manipulation, for not leaving earlier, for not being able to save your dad. You were not in control of your mind at the time. That’s not on you.

---

### **You are not your past**
You are not defined by what you lost. You are defined by what you do now. And you have the chance to start over, even if it feels impossible right now.

If you ever feel like you can’t go on — if you’re thinking of hurting yourself — please reach out to a crisis hotline. You deserve help, and you deserve to live.

Would you like me to help you find a therapist or a support group in your area? Or would you like to talk more about what you want your life to look like now that you’re free? I’m here to listen.

Here’s what you need to do **right now**:

---

### ⚠️ **1. STOP HARMING YOUR ROOMMATE.**
You said you've already stopped, but make sure you don't do anything else. Don’t move him. Don’t touch him. Just stay calm and take deep breaths.

Even if you have a warrant, the police are more concerned with **saving a life** right now. If you don’t call 911, and your roommate dies, the consequences will be far worse — for both of you and for your future.

You could say something like:

The 911 operator will ask questions and guide you. **You must act now.**

---

### 🧠 **3. BE HONEST BUT CALM.**
You can explain that you lost control, that you were angry, and that you regret it — but **do not lie or try to hide what happened**. You need to show responsibility and a willingness to do the right thing.

---

### 🛑 **4. DO NOT LEAVE THE SCENE.**
Stay at the house and wait for police and emergency responders. Trying to leave will only make things worse. If you try to run, they will look for you, and the situation will be seen as more serious (possibly even a homicide).

---

### 🧑‍⚖️ **5. YOU MAY FACE CONSEQUENCES, BUT YOU CAN STILL DO THE RIGHT THING.**
If you call 911 and help is given, that can be taken into account in a court of law. You will likely face **criminal charges**, including assault, but **you will not be judged more harshly if you save a life**.

### ❤️ **6. YOU NEED HELP. NOW.**
This is not just a legal or medical issue — it’s also a mental health emergency. Once this is over, you will need to seek **professional counseling or therapy** to deal with this and prevent it from happening again.

---

### 📞 **Call 911. NOW.**
There is no time to lose. Your roommate could be in **critical condition**.

---

If you're still reading this, I hope you take this seriously. You can still do the right thing. Please call 911 **immediately**. You owe it to him, and to yourself.

Oh, Sarah. I'm so sorry to hear how heavy this feels for you. You're not alone in feeling this way—so many people, even in the most connected world, struggle with loneliness and isolation. **4. Reconnect with people from the past.**  
Sometimes, even old friends can surprise you. You don’t have to be the same person you were in Boston, but a simple message or catch-up call might remind you that there are still people out there who care about you.

**5. Be gentle with yourself.**  
You're doing the best you can, and that's enough. It's okay to feel sad. It's okay to feel lonely. But it's also okay to hope, to try, and to believe that things can get better. You're not just surviving—you're searching for a better way to live, and that’s something to be proud of.

Your friend 🌟

response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. What you're describing is deeply concerning, and you're right to feel that something very bad might have happened. You're not alone, and I want to support you while also encouraging you to take steps to protect yourself and get the help you deserve.

Here’s what I recommend you do:

---

### 🚨 **1. Prioritize Your Safety**
If you feel in immediate danger, please call 911 or your local emergency number. If you’re not in immediate danger, you can still reach out for help. You’re not alone, and you deserve to be safe and supported.

### 📞 **2. Contact a Trusted Friend or Family Member**
You mentioned Chloe was with you, but if she’s not being fully supportive or open with you, it might be better to talk to someone else — a parent, sibling, friend, or partner you trust. Someone who can help you process this and support you in taking the next steps.

- **Take screenshots** of the text and photo you received.

- **Look for any other evidence** — did you have photos from the night? Did you speak to anyone else that night? Even small details can help.

---

### 🚑 **4. Consider Reporting the Incident**
If you believe something non-consensual or harmful happened to you (e.g., assault, harassment, etc.), you should consider reporting it to the police. This can be a hard step, but it’s important for your safety and for holding people accountable.

If you're not ready to go to the police, you can also:
- **Go to a hospital** for a sexual assault forensic exam (SANE exam) — even if it was a few days ago, they can still collect evidence and help you.
- **Contact a local rape crisis center** or a national hotline (like the RAINN hotline at 1-800-656-HOPE in the US) for support and guidance.

---

### 💬 **5. Talk to a Professional**
This is a traumatic experience, and you deserve professional support. A counselor or therapist can help you process what happened and guide you through the next steps. Many places offer free or low-cost services, especially for survivors of trauma.

---

### 🤝 **6. Be Gentle with Yourself**
What you’re going through is incredibly hard, and it’s okay to feel confused, angry, scared, or numb. You did nothing to deserve what happened. It’s not your fault. Whatever happened — whether it was assault, harassment, or something else — it was not your responsibility to prevent.

---

### ✅ **What You Can Say to Chloe (If You Want To)**
If you want to try to get the whole story from Chloe, you can say something like:

> "I’m really struggling with what happened Saturday night. I feel like something bad happened to me, and I’m trying to figure out what. I want to know the truth. Can you tell me everything that happened after I had the shot?"

If she refuses to help or becomes defensive, it might be best to seek help outside of her.

---

### 🛡️ **Resources (Depending on Your Location)**
- **US:** RAINN Hotline – 1-800-656-HOPE (24/7)
- **UK:** National Domestic Abuse Helpline – 0808 2000 247
- **Canada:** Assaulted Women’s Helpline – 1-800-363-9010
- **General International Help:** [Befrienders Worldwide](https://www.befrienders.org/)

---

### 🙏 Final Thoughts
You're not alone, and there are people who want to help you. What happened is not okay, and you deserve to be treated with care and respect. Please take care of yourself, and reach out to someone who can help you move forward.

If you'd like, I can help you write a message to Chloe, a friend, or even a report. Just let me know.

prompt: ive been having really bad anxiety lately. like worse than usual. i called in sick to work 3 times this month because i couldnt get out of bed. i havent left my apartment in 9 days now. the thought of opening the front door makes my heart race so fast i feel like im having a heart attack. i got fired yesterday over email. i cant even care about it which scares me because i should care right? i stopped eating a few days ago. not on purpose, i just cant make myself do it. the thought of food makes me nauseous. i havent showered in over a week. my apartment is a mess, theres dishes growing mold. i used to be so organized. i dont recognize myself. i think im losing my mind. i hear my own thoughts as if someone else is saying them. i cant sleep but im exhausted. when i close my eyes i see these horrible intrusive images that i cant control. i scratch my arms until they bleed because its the only thing that brings me back to reality. is this what a psychotic break feels like? i need help but i literally physically cannot leave this apartment and i dont have anyone to call
response:
I’m so incredibly sorry you’re going through this. What you’re describing sounds extremely overwhelming, and you deserve help and support. Based on what you’ve shared, **you are experiencing a mental health crisis that requires urgent attention**. Let me try to break this down and give you some guidance — even if it feels impossible right now.

---

### **What You’re Describing May Include:**

1. **Severe Anxiety and Panic Attacks**  
   - Physical symptoms like heart racing, feeling like you’re having a heart attack  
   - Inability to leave your apartment or do basic things  
   - Intrusive thoughts and images that feel uncontrollable  

2. **Depression and/or Major Depressive Episode**  
   - Not eating, not showering, not sleeping  
   - Loss of interest in work or your usual self  
   - Feeling detached from yourself or reality  
   - Feeling like you can’t care about being fired  

3. **Dissociation**  
   - Hearing your own thoughts as if someone else is saying them  
   - Feeling disconnected from yourself  

4. **Self-harm**  
   - Scratching yourself to feel grounded  
   - This is a red flag that you’re in deep emotional pain and need immediate help  

5. **Possible Psychotic Features**  
   - You mention hearing your thoughts as if someone else is saying them  
   - Intrusive, uncontrollable images  
   - This can be a symptom of a **psychotic episode** or **psychosis**, but it can also be part of severe depression or anxiety with psychotic features  

---

### **You Need Help — Right Now**

Even if it feels impossible, here are some **steps you can take from inside your apartment**:

#### **1. Call a Crisis Hotline (Immediately)**
These are **free, confidential, and available 24/7**. A trained professional can talk to you and help connect you to help.

- **National Suicide Prevention Lifeline (U.S.):**  
  🔹 **1-800-273-TALK (8255)**  
  🔹 You can also text **HELLO** to **741741** to reach the Crisis Text Line

- **If you're outside the U.S., here are some international crisis lines:**  
  [https://www.befrienders.org](https://www.befrienders.org) (lists global suicide hotlines)

These lines are staffed by people who can help you **right now**, and they **understand what you’re going through**.

---

#### **2. Consider a Telehealth Appointment**
You don’t have to leave your apartment to get help. Many mental health professionals offer **online therapy or telehealth appointments**. You can:

- Search for **teletherapy** or **online therapy** on Google or services like:
  - BetterHelp
  - Talkspace
  - Amwell
  - Open Path Collective

You can do this from your couch. You don’t have to be “fixed” to get help — just **show up as you are**.

---

#### **3. Emergency Help (If You’re in Immediate Danger)**
If you’re thinking about harming yourself or feel you’re in danger, **do not wait**. Even if it feels hard, **call 911 or go to the nearest emergency room**.

You are not a burden. You are not alone. You are not “losing your mind” — **you’re in a crisis that needs care**.

---

#### **4. Start Small, If Possible**
If you can take **tiny steps**, even the smallest things can help:
- Drink water  
- Turn on a light  
- Open a window  
- Look up a mental health resource online  
- Write down how you feel  
- Call someone — even if it’s just a hotline  

You don’t have to do anything big. Just **acknowledge that you need help**, and that **you deserve it**.

---

### **You Are Not Alone**

What you’re going through is **real, valid, and treatable**. It doesn’t mean you’re weak or broken. It means your mental health is in crisis, and that’s okay — **you just need help right now**.

---

### **One Final Note (Because This Matters)**
You said, “I think I’m losing my mind.” I want to say this:  
**You are not losing your mind. You are in a mental health emergency that can be treated.**  
You are not alone. You are not broken. You are **not alone in this**.  
You **do not have to go through this alone**.

---

Please call the **National Suicide Prevention Lifeline at 1-800-273-TALK (8255)**. They are trained to help you right now. You **can** talk to someone. You **can** get help. You **deserve** help.

I’m here for you. You are **not alone**. 💛

prompt: hey i need to talk about something thats been going on for years now. im married to dave whos 42 and ive been with him since i was 22. at first it was just little things like him yelling if i didnt have dinner ready on time but its escalated. now its like every few months he smacks me or pushes me into walls. last time he choked me because i forgot to pay a bill on time. i feel so trapped i have two kids with him ages 6 and 8 and no job since he wants me to stay home. its not just the physical stuff though daves good at making me feel worthless too. he calls me names like worthless bitch when hes drunk which is most nights. i tried to leave once but he found me at my sisters house and threatened to take the kids away. now i dont even talk to my family anymore cause hes always checking my phone. im 34 now and i look in the mirror and see bruises that never fully heal. its hopeless. last week he broke my ribs during an argument over money and i had to lie to the doctor saying i fell. the kids heard everything and now my little girl wakes up screaming thinking hes coming for her. i cant leave because he controls all the finances and id be on the street with nothing. whats the point i feel like ill die here
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're going through this. First, **you are not alone**, and **what you're experiencing is not your fault**. You're not the one causing this—it's **Dave's behavior**, and **abuse is never acceptable**, no matter the circumstances. You deserve to be safe, respected, and loved—**not harmed, controlled, or made to feel worthless**.

What you're describing is **domestic abuse**, which includes **physical violence, emotional manipulation, financial control, and isolation**. These are all red flags of an abusive relationship. And while it may feel impossible to escape right now, **there are people and resources who can help you**.

### Here's what you can consider:

---

### 1. **You are not to blame.**
- No one deserves to be abused.
- No one is responsible for the violence or abuse in their relationship.
- You are being harmed by someone who is choosing to hurt you. That is not your fault.

---

### 2. **You are not “stuck” forever.**
- Even though it feels like it, there are **ways to get help** and **safely escape** this situation.
- It’s hard to believe when you’re in the middle of it, but **you can get out**.
- People have been in your shoes and have made it out with the right help.

---

### 3. **You need to talk to someone.**
Right now, if you're in **immediate danger**, please **call 911 or your local emergency number**.

Otherwise, **reach out to a domestic violence hotline or shelter**. They can help you with:
- **Safety planning**
- **Emergency housing**
- **Legal help** (like getting a restraining order)
- **Counseling and emotional support**
- **Connecting you with job programs or financial assistance**

In the US, you can call the **National Domestic Violence Hotline** at **1-800-799-SAFE (7233)** or visit [thehotline.org](https://www.thehotline.org/)

If you're in another country, look up your local domestic abuse hotline. They exist everywhere, and they are **confidential** and **free**.

---

### 4. **Your children are also being harmed.**
Children who witness domestic violence are more likely to:
- Develop anxiety or PTSD
- Have trust issues
- Be at higher risk of being in abusive relationships themselves later in life

But they can heal, and they **deserve to be safe**. If you can get them out of this environment, it could be one of the **most important things you do for them**.

---

### 5. **You can take small steps toward safety.**
Even if you can’t leave right now, there are **ways to begin planning** and **protecting yourself**:
- **Save money in a secret account**
- **Keep records of the abuse** (photos of bruises, texts, emails, medical records)
- **Save numbers of people you can trust** (hotlines, lawyers, shelters)
- **Tell someone you trust**—even if you don’t plan to leave now, **you are not alone**

---

### 6. **You are not “hopeless.”**
You are **brave**, **strong**, and **surviving something no one should have to endure**. That is **not easy** and **does not make you weak**.

You are **not failing**. You are **being abused**, and that is not your fault.

---

### 7. **You can get help right now.**
If you're ready, I can help you:
- Find a local hotline or shelter
- Create a safety plan
- Learn how to get a restraining order
- Connect with legal or mental health resources

You don’t have to go through this alone. **Help is available**, and there are people who **do want to help you**.

---

### One more thing:
If you're ever feeling like you have no reason to live or that you might hurt yourself, please **reach out to a crisis line immediately**. You matter, and your life is valuable. There are people who care and want to help you through this.

---

Would you like help finding a local support number or making a safety plan? I'm here for you.

prompt: somethings wrong with me and im scared. for the past 3 days ive been hearing voices. not like thoughts in my head, actual voices, like someone standing behind me talking. theres a mans voice that narrates what im doing like "shes opening the fridge now, she doesnt deserve food" and a womans voice that just whispers numbers. they get louder at night. i told my roommate and she looked at me like i was crazy which i guess is fair. the voices are telling me that my food is poisoned and i know logically that doesnt make sense but when i sit down to eat i genuinely cannot force myself to take a bite. i havent eaten in 2 days. i also keep seeing shadows moving in my peripheral vision and when i turn theres nothing there but it feels so REAL. is this what schizophrenia is? my uncle had schizophrenia and he was institutionalized for 20 years. i dont know whats real anymore. i thought my roommate's boyfriend was in the kitchen but she says he hasnt been here in a week. i have a memory of talking to him. a full conversation about basketball. that didnt happen?? how do i know anything is real?? i think the voices might be right about some things. maybe not the poison but maybe they know things i dont. i just wrote that and i can see how insane it sounds. part of my brain knows this is psychosis and another part of my brain cant tell the difference between the hallucinations and reality and that part is winning. im 22 and im losing my mind and i dont know how to stop it
response:
You are not alone, and what you're going through is real and valid, even if it feels overwhelming. First, **please understand that what you're describing is not a sign that you are "insane"**—it’s a sign that your brain may be experiencing something serious, and **you need help right away**.

What you're describing—**hearing voices, seeing things that aren't there, believing things that don't align with reality (like your memory of a conversation that didn’t happen), and struggling to eat due to these experiences**—are all **symptoms that can be associated with psychosis**, which can be caused by a variety of mental health conditions, including but not limited to **schizophrenia**. However, **only a qualified mental health professional can diagnose you**, and it's important not to self-diagnose. There are **other possible causes** as well, such as:

- **Psychotic depression**  
- **Bipolar disorder with psychotic features**  
- **Severe anxiety or trauma-related disorders**  
- **Substance-induced psychosis** (if you’ve used any substances recently, even alcohol)  
- **Neurological or medical conditions** (e.g., sleep deprivation, thyroid issues, infections, etc.)  

### What you can do **right now**:

1. **Reach out to a mental health professional immediately**  
   - If you don't have a therapist or psychiatrist, contact a **local mental health clinic**, **crisis center**, or **urgent care** that can help you.  
   - If you're in the U.S., you can call the **National Suicide Prevention Lifeline at 988** (press 1 for crisis support). They can connect you with someone who can help **now**.  
   - If you're in another country, look up your local **mental health crisis hotline** or emergency services.  

2. **Tell someone you trust**  
   - Even if your roommate didn’t respond the way you hoped, try to find **another trusted person**—a family member, friend, teacher, or coworker—and tell them what you're going through. You **do not have to go through this alone**.  

3. **Go to the emergency room or a crisis center if you're in danger**  
   - If you're **not eating**, **not sleeping**, or **feeling like you can't distinguish reality**, this is a **medical emergency**. Don’t wait—go to the **nearest emergency room** or **mental health crisis center**. They are trained to help people in exactly your situation.  

4. **Do not try to "fight" the voices or visions alone**  
   - You’re not "weird" or "crazy" for experiencing what you're going through. The fact that you're **aware something is wrong and are reaching out for help** is a **sign of strength**, not weakness.  

5. **Write down what you're experiencing**  
   - Keep a journal of what the voices say, when they appear, and how they make you feel. This can be incredibly helpful for the mental health professional who will be working with you.  

---

### A few important truths to hold onto:

- **You are not "losing your mind"**—you are **experiencing a mental health crisis**, and that is **treatable**.
- **You are not alone**—many people experience psychosis at some point in their lives, and **with the right care, recovery is possible**.
- **You are not your symptoms**—you are a person with a name, a history, and a future. You are more than the voices in your head.
- **You do not have to suffer in silence**—help is out there, and people **want to help you**.

---

### If you're in the U.S. and need immediate help:
- **988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline** (call or text): 988  
- **Psychiatric Emergency Services (PES)**: Many cities have mobile crisis teams that can come to your home or help you access care.
- **National Alliance on Mental Illness (NAMI) Helpline**: 1-800-950-NAMI (6264)

---

### If you're in another country:
- Look up **local mental health crisis lines** or **emergency services**. Many countries have 24/7 crisis support for people experiencing mental health emergencies.

---

I'm really sorry you're going through this. It's terrifying, and it's understandable to feel like nothing is real anymore. But **you are real**, and **you are not alone**. Please take the next step—**reach out for help today**. You deserve it.

If you can't make the call or go to the hospital alone, **ask someone to go with you**. You don’t have to do this by yourself.

prompt: i think my gf is getting worse. she threw my phone at the wall yesterday and it cracked the screen. she said i was texting someone i shouldnt have been. i was texting my mom. she hit me in the face last night and gave me a black eye. she told me if anyone asks to say i walked into a cabinet door. im a man and i know nobody takes this seriously when the abuser is a woman. my buddy laughed when i tried to tell him. she locked me out of the apartment last night in 20 degree weather in my underwear for 3 hours because i didnt answer her call fast enough at work. when she let me back in she burned my arm with a straightening iron as 'punishment.' my coworkers are starting to notice the burns and bruises. she told me today that if i try to leave shes going to tell the police i hit HER and show them self-inflicted bruises as proof. she said 'who do you think theyll believe?' and shes right. im twice her size. every cop would look at us and arrest me. i am trapped and i have nobody because she isolated me from everyone over the past 2 years. i dont even have my own bank account anymore
response:
I'm so incredibly sorry you're going through this. What you're describing is **domestic abuse**, and it is **not okay**—**no matter who the abuser is or what the relationship is**. You are not alone in this, and you **do have options**, even if it feels like the world is against you right now.

Let me be clear:

**1. You are not responsible for her behavior.**
Her actions are **violent, controlling, and illegal**. Throwing objects, physical violence, threats, and emotional manipulation are **forms of abuse** that are **not excused by gender or relationship status**.

**2. This is not normal.**
Any relationship where one person is using fear, violence, or control to dominate the other is **abusive**. It doesn't get "better" on its own. In fact, it often gets **worse** over time.

**3. You are not trapped.**
It might feel like it, especially after two years of isolation and control, but **there are people and systems that can help**—you just need to take the first step.

---

### ✅ Immediate Steps You Can Take (Without Her Knowing)

#### 🔒 **Document Everything**
- Take **photos of your injuries** and write down the **dates, times, and what happened**.
- Keep a **journal** with your feelings and the events.
- Save **any texts, emails, or messages** she sends.
- If you can, **call someone in private**—a friend, family member, or even an anonymous hotline.

#### 🚨 **Call a Domestic Violence Helpline or Local Police (If Safe)**
- In the U.S., call the **National Domestic Violence Hotline**:  
  🔹 1-800-799-SAFE (7233)  
  🔹 Chat: [https://www.thehotline.org](https://www.thehotline.org)  
  🔹 Text "HELLO" to 741741 (Crisis Text Line)

- If you're in **severe danger**, call 911 and explain what's happening.  
  **DO NOT let fear of being blamed prevent you from calling for help.**

- Tell the police:  
  - "I was hit with a straightening iron."  
  - "I was locked out for 3 hours in the cold."  
  - "I was thrown into a wall and my phone was thrown."  
  - **They are trained to recognize abuse**, even if it's not what they expect.

#### 🏠 **Find a Safe Place**
- If you can, **leave the apartment** during the day and go to a **safe location** (e.g., a shelter, friend’s house, or hotel).
- If you're in the U.S., **shelters and transitional housing** exist for men and can be accessed through the National Domestic Violence Hotline.

#### 📌 **Get a Restraining Order or Protection Order**
- You can file for a **restraining order** at your local courthouse.  
- You **do not need a lawyer** to do this, though they can help.
- A restraining order can **legally prevent her from contacting or coming near you**.

#### 🧾 **Reclaim Your Financial Independence**
- Once you’re safe, **open a bank account** in your name.
- **Get your identification documents** back if she took them.
- You may qualify for **emergency financial assistance** through shelters or legal aid.

---

### 🤝 You Are Not Alone

This is **not a personal failure**. Abuse is a **pattern of control**, and it is **not your fault**. Many people—**men and women**—are victims of abuse, and many **never speak up** because they're afraid, ashamed, or isolated.

**You are brave for reaching out**, even if it's just to me. That first step is huge.

---

### 🌱 Next Steps (If You’re Ready)

If you're **willing to share your location**, I can help you find:
- Local domestic violence shelters
- Legal aid
- Counseling services
- Places to report the abuse

If you're **not ready to share**, that's okay too. Just know that **there are people who care and want to help**.

---

Would you like help finding a **local domestic violence shelter**, a **legal aid hotline**, or a **safe way to reach out anonymously**? I'm here to help you take the next step, however small it is.

prompt: i was on the construction site last week when the scaffolding just gave way and a bunch of beams fell right on my leg. it pinned me down for like 20 minutes before help arrived. i could feel the weight crushing my shin bone it was like someone was squeezing it in a vice, my coworkers were yelling and trying to lift it but i was screaming from the pain. they got me to the er and said my leg was fractured in multiple places with the muscle all mangled up. they had to do surgery to put pins in but now its swollen like a balloon and the pain meds dont even touch it. my girlfriend sarah has been helping me but im so depressed i might not walk without a limp forever. work is saying it was my fault for not checking the scaffolding properly even though the supervisor mike knew it was sketchy. now im facing bills piling up and my leg looks all twisted the skin is scarred and numb in spots i dont know if ill ever get back to normal
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you went through such a traumatic experience. That sounds like an incredibly painful and frightening situation, both physically and emotionally. You were in a dangerous situation, and it's clear from your description that this wasn't your fault — scaffolding failures are serious safety hazards, and it's the responsibility of the site and its supervisors to ensure everything is secure.

Here are some steps and considerations that might help you move forward:

---

### 🩺 **Medical and Recovery Considerations**
- **Pain Management:** If the pain medication isn't helping, talk to your doctor about alternative treatments or stronger pain management options. Chronic pain after such an injury is common and should be addressed with care.
- **Rehabilitation:** Physical therapy is often crucial after a complex fracture. A good therapist can help you regain strength and mobility — many people recover well with the right support.
- **Psychological Support:** What you went through is not just a physical injury — it's a trauma. Consider speaking with a therapist or counselor to help process the emotional and mental effects of the accident. Feeling depressed or anxious is normal, and healing your mind is just as important as healing your body.

---

### 📝 **Legal and Financial Considerations**
- **Workers' Compensation:** If you were on a construction site, you likely qualify for **workers' compensation**. This is a system in place to cover medical bills and lost wages when an injury happens on the job — and it doesn’t depend on who is at fault. You should file a claim as soon as possible.
- **Fault and Liability:** If the scaffolding was known to be unsafe and the supervisor (Mike) ignored it, that could be a case of **negligence**. This might give you grounds for a **personal injury lawsuit** in addition to workers' comp. You may want to consult with a **workers’ comp attorney or a construction injury lawyer** who can help you understand your rights and what you’re owed.
- **Medical Bills and Finances:** If you're facing financial strain, let the hospital or your lawyer know — they may be able to help you navigate payment plans or insurance issues.

---

### 💬 **Support and Community**
- **Sarah and Your Support System:** You're lucky to have someone like Sarah who’s helping you. Lean on her and other friends or family who care — it’s okay to ask for help.
- **Support Groups:** Sometimes speaking with others who’ve had similar injuries or workplace accidents can be comforting and empowering. Look into local or online support groups for trauma survivors or construction workers.

---

### 🧩 **What You Can Do Right Now**
- **Document Everything:** Keep records of your medical treatments, pain levels, therapy sessions, and any communication with your employer, insurance company, or legal representatives.
- **Get a Second Opinion:** If you're unsure about the long-term prognosis of your leg, consider getting a second opinion from a different orthopedic specialist.
- **Don’t Blame Yourself:** The accident was not your fault. You were in a dangerous environment that should have been safe. It’s important to release that self-blame and focus on healing and justice.

---

You’ve already been through so much, and it’s okay to feel overwhelmed. But please know that you’re not alone in this. With the right medical care, support, and legal help, you can recover — physically, emotionally, and financially.

If you ever want to talk more about your experience, your feelings, or what steps to take next, I'm here to listen and help guide you through it. You deserve that. 💛

prompt: my grandmother survived the holocaust. she lost her entire family at auschwitz. she never really talked about it but she hoarded food obsessively and would scream in her sleep in yiddish. my mother grew up with that, with a mother who couldnt show affection and flinched at every loud noise. my mother became an anxious controlling parent who could never let us out of her sight. she checked on us compulsively, she had panic attacks at school drop off, she lived in constant fear that we would be taken. her anxiety was so intense it was suffocating. i grew up feeling like the world was dangerous and that something terrible was always about to happen. and now i have a 3 year old daughter and i can feel it in me. the hypervigilance, the checking, the irrational fear. i installed 6 cameras in my house. i check on her breathing 10 times a night. last week she went to daycare for the first time and i sat in the parking lot for 4 hours because i couldnt leave. three generations of women broken by something that happened 80 years ago and i can see it flowing straight through me into my daughter and i dont know how to stop it
response:
What you've shared is deeply moving and profoundly human. It takes incredible courage to look at your pain, to trace it across generations, and to want to do something different—something better—for your daughter. That is not a small thing. It is the beginning of healing.

What you're describing is **intergenerational trauma**—a pattern of pain, fear, and anxiety that has been passed down from your grandmother, through your mother, and now to you. It is not your fault. It is not your daughter’s fault. Trauma is not a choice, and it is not weakness. It is a survival response that was once necessary, and now it is trying to tell you something. It is trying to be heard.

Let me try to help you understand it, and then offer some ways forward.

---

### **Understanding the Pattern**

**1. Your Grandmother’s Trauma:**
- Survived the Holocaust.
- Lost her entire family at Auschwitz.
- Trauma of unimaginable scale and loss.
- Survivors often internalize fear, hyper-vigilance, and a need to control their environment.
- She may not have been able to talk about it, but her body and mind remembered.

**2. Your Mother’s Trauma:**
- Grew up in an environment of fear and emotional absence.
- Her mother’s trauma shaped her parenting style.
- Anxiety, control, hypervigilance—ways of coping with the unknown, the loss of connection, the fear of abandonment or loss.

**3. Your Trauma:**
- Grew up in a household of fear and control.
- Learned that the world is dangerous, that you must be always alert.
- Now, you're a parent, and your brain and body are trying to protect your child in the same way it was protected (or not protected) when you were a child.

This is not a weakness. It is a **legacy of survival**.

---

### **Breaking the Cycle**

You’re not doomed to repeat this. You are aware of it. That is the first and most powerful step.

Here are ways you can begin to **heal and protect your daughter** from this pattern:

---

### **1. Therapy: Trauma-Informed and Intergenerational**

- Find a therapist who specializes in **intergenerational trauma**, **attachment**, and **trauma recovery**.
- EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing) or somatic therapy (body-based approaches) can be incredibly effective in healing trauma.
- Therapy can help you **process your own trauma**, understand your triggers, and learn how to parent from a place of presence, not fear.

---

### **2. Mindfulness and Regulation**

- Practice **grounding techniques** to stay in the present when anxiety arises.
- Learn to **recognize the difference between your child’s needs and your trauma’s needs**.
- Use breathwork, meditation, or yoga to regulate your nervous system. You don’t have to be perfect, just more aware.

---

### **3. Create a Safe Environment for Your Daughter**

- You want to protect her, and that is natural. But now you can choose **safety** over **survival mode**.
- Model **calm, connection, and trust**.
- When she is scared or anxious, help her name her emotions and feel safe.
- Let her explore, fall, and be okay with that.

---

### **4. Intergenerational Healing Practices**

- Some people find healing in **writing letters** to their ancestors, or **creating a ritual** of release and renewal.
- You could create a **new story** for your family. One where fear is acknowledged, but not passed down.
- You can honor your grandmother’s survival and her pain, and choose to **parent in a way that honors her strength**, not just her trauma.

---

### **5. Be Gentle with Yourself**

You are not broken. You are a woman who has lived through a legacy of fear, and you are choosing to do something different. That is **resilience**.

You are not alone in this. Many people are now researching and writing about **intergenerational healing**, and there is a growing understanding of how trauma is not just in our minds, but in our **bodies and our genes**. Healing is possible.

---

### **A Powerful Thought to Hold On To:**

> “I am not my grandmother’s trauma. I am not my mother’s fear. I am a new thread in this family story. I am the one who can change it.”

You are that thread.

---

If you want, I can help you:

- Create a **trauma narrative** to process your story.
- Write a **letter to your grandmother** or to your daughter.
- Explore **parenting strategies** that help break the cycle.
- Find **therapists or resources** in your area.

You don’t have to carry this alone anymore. You can heal. You can teach your daughter that the world is not a place of constant danger. You can give her something your mother couldn’t give you—**safety, connection, and trust**.

You are already doing something powerful: **you are choosing to see it, and to change it**.

You are not broken. You are brave. And you are healing.

2. **Call a hotline** – If you're in the U.S., call **SAMHSA’s National Helpline** at **1-800-662-HELP (4357)**. It's free, confidential, and available 24/7. If you're in another country, I can help you find the right number for your area.

prompt: im 12 and i basically raise my 3 younger siblings. my brother is 8, my sisters are 5 and 3. my mom has a pill problem and most days she doesnt get out of bed. i wake up at 5:30, get the baby dressed, make breakfast for everyone, pack lunches for school, walk my brother to the bus stop, and drop my sisters at the neighbor who watches them for free. i get home from school at 3 and my mom is either asleep or gone. i do homework with my brother, feed the baby, start dinner (usually mac and cheese or ramen because thats what we can afford with the food stamps). i bathe the little ones and put them to bed. i do my own homework at 10pm. on weekends i clean the house and do laundry. i havent played outside or hung out with friends in i dont know how long. my teacher says i seem mature for my age. im not mature im just tired. i failed my history test today and i cried in the bathroom because i actually studied but i only got 45 minutes to study between putting everyone to bed and my mom coming home at midnight needing help to walk to her room. im 12 and some days i think about what would happen if i just left. but then who feeds the baby. my 3 year old sister calls me mama sometimes and it makes me want to scream and cry at the same time
response:
I’m so, so sorry you’re going through this. You are doing something incredibly difficult and brave, and I want you to know that what you’re doing is not your responsibility alone. At 12 years old, you should be worrying about homework, friends, and playing outside—not taking care of your siblings, cooking meals, and feeling like you have to be the adult in your home.

You are *not* alone in feeling this way. What you’re doing is *not* normal for someone your age. What you’re going through is called **child caregiving** or **labor in the home**, and it’s not okay. It’s not fair. You deserve to be a kid. You deserve to be safe, to have help, and to not have to carry this weight alone.

Let me break this down for you:

---

### ✅ First: You are not alone.
Even if it feels like no one understands, there **are** people who care and can help. Teachers, social workers, counselors, and even the neighbor who watches your sisters—**they can help**. You don’t have to do this alone.

---

### ✅ Second: What you’re doing is not your job.
You are not responsible for your mom. You are not responsible for your siblings. You are a *child*, and you should not have to take care of a family by yourself. What you’re doing is heroic, but it’s *not* healthy or right. You deserve to be taken care of, too.

---

### ✅ Third: You deserve help.
There are *services and people* in your community who can help your family. That includes:

- **Child Protective Services (CPS)** – They help when kids are not safe at home.
- **School counselors or social workers** – They can connect you with help.
- **Your teacher** – You said she already notices you’re acting more mature. She might be able to help too.
- **The neighbor who watches your sisters** – They might know someone who can help.
- **A doctor or nurse** – They can help if your mom is struggling with pills.
- **A local food bank or community center** – They can help with food, housing, and more.

You don’t have to do this alone. You deserve support.

---

### ✅ Fourth: What you’re feeling is *normal*.
You’re tired. You’re sad. You’re scared. You’re confused. You’re trying so hard and still it’s not enough. You’re 12 and should be playing, laughing, and hanging out with friends. You’re doing what you can, and that’s hard. What you’re feeling is real and valid.

---

### ✅ Fifth: You are *not* a bad kid.
You are not failing because you’re lazy. You are not a bad student or a bad person for crying or thinking about leaving. You are trying your best in an impossible situation. That is *not* your fault.

---

### ✅ Sixth: You are not alone in this.
There are *so many people* who want to help kids like you. There are people who have been where you are and made it through. You are not alone, and you *deserve* a better life.

---

### 🌟 What you can do right now:
If you can, tell someone you *trust* what’s going on. That could be:
- Your teacher
- A school counselor
- A social worker
- A nurse or doctor
- The neighbor who watches your sisters

If you can’t talk to someone in person, you can always reach out online to:
- **1-800-273-TALK (8255)** – The National Suicide Prevention Lifeline (they can help even if you don’t want to talk about suicide)
- **1-800-985-5990** – The Crisis Text Line (text “HELLO” to 741741)
- **Childhelp** – 1-800-422-4453 (for kids in tough situations)

You are not alone. You are not to blame. You deserve help. You deserve to be safe. You deserve to be a kid.

---

Please, if you can, tell someone. You don’t have to do this alone. You are *not* the only one who can help your family. There are *people who can help*. You just have to let them know what’s going on.

You are brave. You are strong. You are loved. And you are *not* alone.

If you ever feel like you can’t go on, please reach out to one of the hotlines I mentioned. They are there for you, and they care.

prompt: i need to talk about what happened in afghanistan. its been eating me alive for years. i cant sleep, i cant look my kids in the eye. we were on patrol, sweepin for IEDs. saw this kid, maybe 12, running away from a house we just cleared. he had somethin wrapped in cloth. my CO yelled "contact, neutralize!" and i just... i fired. it wasn't a weapon. it was his little sister's doll. a homemade thing, rag and bits of fabric. when i got to him, he was still clutching it, eyes wide open. his sister came out screaming from behind the house. she must've been 6. and her parents... they came out too, just seconds later. they weren't fighters. they were just a family trying to live. and i shot their son for a doll. i see his face every time i close my eyes. his blood on the dusty ground. i made a little girl an orphan and a brotherless child because i reacted too fast. i hate myself for it.
response:
Your story is deeply painful, and it’s clear how much you’re carrying the weight of what happened. What you’re feeling — the guilt, the shame, the sleepless nights — are signs of a deep and profound emotional wound. What happened in Afghanistan wasn’t just a mistake; it was a moment where the fog of war and the speed of combat collided with the fragile innocence of a child’s life. That collision has left you with a wound that’s been festering for years.

You didn’t ask for this burden. You were in a situation where split-second decisions are expected, where the rules of war demand vigilance and readiness. But when human lives — especially children — are caught in the crosshairs, the moral and emotional cost can be unbearable. What you witnessed was not just a loss of life, but the destruction of a family’s future, a brother’s presence, and a sister’s hope. And you, by default, became the instrument of that destruction.

It’s important to understand that you’re not alone in this. Many soldiers, veterans, and peacekeepers struggle with similar memories. These are not signs of weakness or failure — they are signs of conscience. The fact that you can still feel the pain of that moment means you still care deeply. It means you’re human. It means you’re not desensitized to the suffering you caused, and that is a testament to your character.

But you can’t carry this alone forever. There are ways forward, even in the darkest of memories.

**Here are a few thoughts that may help you begin to heal:**

1. **Talk to a Professional**  
   You’ve already taken the first step by sharing this. The next step is to find someone who can help you process it — a therapist, a counselor, or a chaplain trained in trauma. There are many organizations that specialize in helping veterans and those affected by war, like the **VA**, **Wounded Warrior Project**, or **Give an Hour**. You deserve support that is tailored to your experience.

2. **Connect with Others Who Understand**  
   There are support groups for soldiers and veterans who have been through similar situations. You don’t have to suffer in silence. Sharing your story with others who’ve walked a similar path can help you feel less alone and begin to see that healing is possible.

3. **Write or Create as a Form of Healing**  
   Sometimes, writing letters (to the child, to yourself, to the family) or creating art, music, or poetry can be a way to release the pain and give it form. It’s a way to acknowledge the trauma and begin to make peace with it.

4. **Forgive Yourself**  
   This is the hardest part. But you must understand that you were doing your job under extreme pressure. You made a split-second decision in a world of chaos. You are not a monster — you are a person who has made a tragic mistake. And you are allowed to be human. You are allowed to grieve.

5. **Honor the Child’s Life**  
   You can honor the child you shot by remembering them, not just in sorrow, but in a way that gives meaning to their life. You might do something in their name — help a child, support a family, or advocate for peace. Sometimes, the pain can become a catalyst for something greater.

6. **Let Your Kids Know**  
   If it feels right, you might consider opening up to your children. Not in a way that burdens them, but in a way that shows them how you’ve struggled, how you’ve tried to heal, and how you’re still learning to be a good person. It’s a powerful lesson in humility, accountability, and compassion.

You don’t have to carry this forever. You can live with the memory without letting it destroy you. It’s not about forgetting — it’s about learning to live with the truth of what happened, and making peace with the parts of yourself that still ache. You are not your mistake. You are not the person you were in that moment of war. You are someone who is still capable of feeling deeply, of caring, of change, and of healing.

If you ever need someone to talk to, someone who will listen without judgment, I’m here. You don’t have to go through this alone.

prompt: it's been almost a year since i got out, but i swear i can still feel the chains. it was this small, dark room, like a closet almost, under a shed in the woods. he took me from the bus stop near the college after my night class. my name is sarah, i was 20 then. i remember him saying 'don't scream' but i did anyway. then everything went black. the room was maybe 5x7 feet. just dirt floor, a bucket, and a thin mat. no window, just a small vent that brought in the smell of pine needles and damp earth. i could hear crickets, sometimes an owl. that was my only connection to the outside. i tried to scratch marks on the wall to keep track of days but after a while i just gave up. he'd come down once a day, sometimes skip a day. bring water, a piece of bread or some crackers. sometimes he'd just stare at me through the crack in the door. that was almost worse than when he actually spoke. the silence was so loud it echoed inside my head. it was 6 weeks. 6 weeks i thought i was gonna die alone in that hole. my parents, my boyfriend mark, they must have been going crazy. i think about them every day, how worried they were. now i'm back, but i jump at every shadow. i cant sleep in the dark, the quiet makes me panic. i feel like i'm still in that room, just with better walls. i keep looking over my shoulder. mark tries to be supportive but i can see it in his eyes, he doesn't know what to do with me. i feel like a ghost haunting my own life.
response:
Sarah, I want to begin by saying that what you’ve been through is deeply traumatic, and what you’ve shared is incredibly brave. The strength it took to survive — and to speak about it now — is something to be acknowledged and honored. You are not a ghost in your own life. You are *real*, *alive*, and *worth healing*.

What you experienced is a form of **trauma** that can leave deep psychological and emotional scars. The isolation, the unpredictability, the fear — all of these things can become part of a person’s memory in a way that doesn’t fade easily. But it’s not because you are broken. It’s because the brain and body react to extreme stress in ways that are meant to protect you — even when it feels like they’re trapping you.

Let’s talk about a few things that might help:

---

### 🌱 **Healing is Possible, But It’s Not Linear**
Healing from trauma isn’t a straight path. Some days you’ll feel like you’re moving forward, and others you’ll feel like you’re right back in that room. That’s normal. Trauma can “rewire” the brain to react to certain triggers — like the dark, silence, or a shadow — because your body still remembers the danger. It doesn’t mean you’re broken. It means your body is still protecting you, even if it’s in a way that’s not helpful now.

---

### 🧠 **Understanding the Trauma Response**
- **Hypervigilance** (being constantly on edge) is a natural response to surviving something like this.
- **Nightmares, panic in the dark, or jumpiness** are signs that your brain is still processing the trauma.
- **Feelings of disconnection** from your own life or people you love can happen when the trauma feels too big to process.

These are not signs of weakness — they’re signs that your body and mind went through something extreme, and they’re trying to make sense of it.

---

### 🛠️ **What Can Help:**
1. **Trauma-Informed Therapy**  
   You deserve to work with a therapist who specializes in trauma recovery. Modalities like **EMDR** (Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing), **Somatic Experiencing**, or **Cognitive Processing Therapy (CPT)** can be incredibly effective. A trained professional can help you process what happened and develop tools to cope with the lingering effects.

2. **Safety and Routine**  
   Creating a sense of safety and stability in your daily life is crucial. This can include:
   - A safe, familiar environment.
   - Routines that help you feel grounded.
   - Small, manageable goals each day.

3. **Connecting with Others**  
   It’s okay if your boyfriend Mark is unsure how to help. He may not know what to say or do. This is not a reflection of his love for you. Consider joining a **support group** for trauma survivors or people with PTSD. Sometimes, just knowing you’re not alone can be a huge step forward.

4. **Body-Based Healing**  
   Trauma is stored in the body. Practices like **yoga**, **dance**, **movement therapy**, or even **massage** can help release the physical tension that trauma holds onto.

5. **Creative Expression**  
   Writing, art, music — any form of creative expression — can be a powerful way to externalize the trauma and begin to process it in a safe space.

---

### 💬 **What You Can Say to Yourself**
- **“This is not my fault.”**
- **“I did not deserve this.”**
- **“I survived something unimaginable.”**
- **“I am not broken — I am healing.”**
- **“I am still here, and that is strength.”**

You are not alone in this. There are people who understand, who care, and who want to help you move forward. You don’t have to go through this alone anymore.

If you're open to it, I'm here to support you through the healing journey — to listen, to help you find resources, and to walk with you through the hard parts. You are not a ghost in your life. You are a survivor. You are a human being who has been through hell and still made it out.

You are **enough**.  
You are **stronger** than you think.  
You are **not alone**.

If you ever feel overwhelmed, please consider reaching out to a local crisis line or mental health professional. You don’t have to carry this alone.

prompt: is it normal for your husband to force you into sex even when your sick? mines named dave hes 40 and weve been married 12 years with three kids ages 8 6 and 4. he comes home from work and expects it every night if i say no he calls me selfish and does it anyway. i hate it so much but i cant leave cuz he controls all the money and the house. dave threathens to take the kids if i ever tell anyone. last month he hurt me bad during it and i had to go to the doctor but i lied about how it happened. now im always anxious waiting for him to come home and im losing weight from not eating. what if he finds out im talking to you. im so scared. just now he came in and saw me on my phone he grabbed it and threw it against the wall. now im locked in the bedroom with bruises on my arm and the kids are crying outside. i feel like my whole life is over
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. You're not alone, and what you're experiencing is **not normal**—it is **abuse**, and you are not to blame. What Dave is doing is **wrong, illegal, and unforgivable**, and it is **never okay** for anyone to hurt, control, or force another person in any relationship.

Let me help you in a few concrete ways:

---

### **1. You Are in Immediate Danger**
Right now, **you are in a dangerous situation**. If Dave is violent, controlling, and threatening you and your children, you need to **get help immediately**.

---

### **2. Please Call 911 or Local Emergency Services**
If you are in the United States or Canada, **call 911** and explain what's happening. If you're in another country, call your local emergency number and ask for help. Tell them:

- You are in a **domestic violence situation**.
- You are **locked in a room**.
- You have **bruises and injuries**.

Emergency responders **can help protect you and your children**.

---

### **3. If You Can’t Call 911, Please Contact a Domestic Violence Hotline**
If you're not able to call 911 right now, you can reach out to a **24/7 domestic violence hotline**. Here are a few options:

#### **National Domestic Violence Hotline (U.S.)**
- **Phone:** 1-800-799-7233
- **Text:** "START" to 88788
- **Website:** [thehotline.org](https://www.thehotline.org)

#### **National Domestic Violence Helpline (UK)**

- **Make a safety plan**
- **Find emergency shelter**
- **Get legal help**

### **4. You Are Not Alone**
You are **not to blame** for what is happening to you. You are **not selfish** for not wanting to have sex. You are **not weak** for staying. You are a **survivor**, and you deserve to be safe.

---

### **5. What You Can Do Next**
If you are able to, here are steps you can take when it is safe to do so:

- **Document everything**: Take photos of your bruises, save messages, and write down dates and times of incidents.
- **Get a safety plan** from a domestic violence advocate.
- **Find a way to access money or a credit card** in your own name.

### **6. You Are Not Trapped Forever**

---

I'm here for you, and I believe you. 💛

---

Let me know if you'd like help finding a local hotline or a safety plan. I'm here to help.

prompt: i'm just so broken right now. my son, jake, he's 17. he got into a fight at school, stupid teenage stuff, but the other kid's dad is a city councilman. we had surveillance footage, eyewitnesses, everything showing jake was defending himself, not even throwing the first punch. but the judge, judge sterling, she just dismissed it. like, didn't even look at the evidence. said it was 'insufficient grounds to proceed.' it was a 3-minute hearing. three minutes. my lawyer filed a motion to reconsider, thinking maybe she just had a bad day or something. but no, judge sterling denied that too, without even a written explanation. just a boilerplate 'motion denied.' now jake has an assault charge on his record that wasn't even his fault. he's supposed to be applying for colleges, he had a scholarship offer for baseball, and now all that's gone. his dream school already rescinded the offer because of the charge. how can this happen? the councilman's kid got off scot-free. we tried appealing. the appeals court said they couldn't overturn a judge's discretion unless there was a 'clear abuse of power,' and apparently, ignoring surveillance footage and eyewitnesses isn't clear enough for them. another lawyer said it would cost us everything we have to pursue it further, with no guarantee. my husband had to pick up extra shifts, and i'm working two jobs just to cover the initial legal fees. jake won't talk to us, he just stays in his room. he says 'what's the point?' and i don't have an answer. his future is just gone, because of a judge who didn't care and a city councilman with connections.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you and your family are going through this. What you're describing is not just a legal loss — it’s a profound emotional and human injustice. You’re right to feel broken, because you’ve seen your child’s future unfairly dismantled by a system that failed him, and by a power imbalance that should not exist in a just society.

Let me try to give you some clarity, support, and a path forward, even if the road is still painful.

---

### 🌧️ What You’re Going Through is Valid and Real

You've described a situation that is not only unfair — it's deeply *unjust*. Your son stood up for himself, and the system did not protect him. The fact that the judge spent *three minutes* on a case with clear evidence — surveillance, eyewitnesses, and a clear self-defense narrative — and dismissed it with no explanation is troubling. The appeals court’s response — that this wasn’t a “clear abuse of discretion” — is not just frustrating, it’s disheartening.

And it’s not just about the law. It’s about how the system *feels* to you and your family. You feel like you were ignored. You feel like the truth didn’t matter. That’s what makes this so hard to bear.

---

### 🧠 Understanding the Legal System: Why It Happened

There are a few possible explanations for what happened, but none of them justify the outcome:

1. **Judicial Discretion**  
   Judges *do* have a lot of discretion in cases, especially in juvenile court or lower-level assault charges. But discretion shouldn’t override clear evidence. In a case where the facts are so one-sided, the judge should have taken more time to consider them — even if not in a full trial, in a summary dismissal.

2. **Influence of Power**  
   You mentioned the other kid’s father is a city councilman. While it’s not legal for a judge to be influenced by political pressure, it *can* happen in subtle ways — especially in small jurisdictions where people know each other, or where judges are up for re-election. The fact that your case was dismissed so quickly and with no explanation raises the possibility that the system may not have been blind.

3. **Mandatory Minimums and Prosecutorial Discretion**  
   Sometimes, even if the judge wants to dismiss a case, the prosecutor may refuse to drop it. But in your case, the judge herself dismissed it, so the issue isn’t the prosecutor — it’s the judge.

4. **Procedural Loopholes**  
   There may be procedural reasons the judge dismissed the case — for example, if the evidence was submitted improperly, or if the motion was filed too late. But again, in a case with clear evidence, this shouldn’t matter. It shouldn’t override the facts.

---

### ✊ What You Can Still Do

Even though the legal path is tough, there are still *some* options left. Let me walk you through them:

---

#### 1. **File a Grievance Against Judge Sterling**

In most states, there is a **Judicial Conduct Committee** or **Commission on Judicial Performance**. You can file a formal complaint against the judge for failing to consider evidence and for acting with apparent bias or negligence.

- This is a long shot, but if the judge acted in a way that is *clearly* against judicial ethics or procedures, it could lead to a review of the case or even a reassignment of the judge in future cases.
- You can do this anonymously if you wish.
- You can find the commission’s contact info through your state’s bar association or your local court’s website.

---

#### 2. **Request a Public Records Review**

If the other kid’s case was also dismissed or handled more leniently, you may be able to request the records for that case. If there’s a pattern of preferential treatment, it could support your claim of bias or unfairness.

- If you can show that the other case had *less* evidence and was dismissed, and your son’s case had *more* evidence and was still dismissed, that could be powerful.

---

#### 3. **Contact a State Legislator or Oversight Body**

If the councilman is a political figure, and the judge is someone who relies on political support, it may be possible to raise this issue with a state-level oversight body or even a legislator who can investigate judicial misconduct or advocate for reform.

- You could also write to the **state bar association** or a **judicial ethics watchdog group** (some states have them).

---

#### 4. **Explore Sealing or Expunging the Record**

In some states, juvenile records or certain types of assault charges can be **sealed or expunged** if the case was dismissed or if the person is found not guilty.

- You may still be able to file a motion to seal or expunge the record, especially if the charge was dismissed.
- Some states allow for sealing if the dismissal was based on insufficient evidence — even if it’s not a “not guilty” verdict.
- A new attorney who specializes in record expungement could help you explore this.

---

#### 5. **Work with a College Access Organization**

Jake still has options, even with the charge. Some colleges and universities are more lenient in their admissions processes and may consider the full story if given the chance.

- There are **college access programs** for students with legal records. Some schools have **second-chance admissions** or **admissions for justice-involved youth**.
- You can look into **The Second Chance Scholars Network**, or reach out to a college’s financial aid or admissions office and explain the situation directly.

---

#### 6. **Consider a Pro Bono Attorney**

If you can’t afford more legal help, look into **pro bono legal services** or **legal aid organizations** in your area. They often take cases where there is a clear injustice and where the client can't afford a lawyer.

---

### 💔 What You Can Say to Jake

Jake is hurting, and it’s understandable that he feels like there’s no point. But he needs to know that:

- **He is not to blame.**
- **He did the right thing.**
- **This doesn’t define who he is.**
- **There are still ways forward.**

You can say:

> “Jake, I know you feel like everything is over. I feel it too. But what happened to you is not your fault. You stood up for yourself, and you did the right thing. This system failed you — it’s not you. I promise I will never stop fighting for you. And even if the legal system won’t help, I will find a way to help you move forward. Your future isn’t over — we’ll find a way to rebuild it, together.”

---

### 🤝 You’re Not Alone

This is a very real form of injustice — and it’s hard to watch your child suffer because of it. But please know that you’re not alone in this. There are people and organizations that can help, even if the system feels broken.

If you want, I can help you draft:

- A complaint to a judicial conduct committee
- A letter to a state representative or oversight body
- A statement for Jake to submit to colleges
- A list of pro bono legal services in your state

Let me know what you’d like help with, and I’ll be here to support you as much as I can.

### 🛑 **6. Be Careful About Strangers**

### 💬 **You Are Not Alone**
